title: How to Find a Laboratory Internship in France
text:
How to Find a Laboratory Internship in France
Finding an internship in a laboratory (lab) in France is not an easy thing, especially in a reputable lab. I’ll give some tips according to my personal experience. First of all, make sure you really want to work in academia, which means that you were not forced to work in academia, e.g., you could not find an internship in industry and thought maybe an internship in a lab is easier to find. This is not the case we want to talk about.
Once you have made up your mind to work in a lab, then you can start to contact the professor you want to work with. There are many ways to get in touch with them, such as sending an email or finding someone you both know to help you. These two are the most popular ways to find an internship in a lab, especially in France, according to my personal experience.
Let me talk about the second way first. Finding someone you both know is the most direct way, but not necessarily the most possible way. In this case, whether you can find an internship almost only depends on the relationship between you and the person you find, and between that person and the professor. If the person you find is very familiar with the professor, and he has a good impression of you, not necessarily “very good”, and he is willing to say a few nice things about you to the professor, then it’s done, you get the job. In this case, you do not even need to send your CV and grades to the professor. But there is another case: if the person you find is not very familiar with you and says something general to the professor, then it is even worse than directly sending an email to the professor yourself.
If you cannot find anyone who is willing to help you, e.g., you just came to France one year ago to start your Master’s program, the easiest way is to send emails to professors you are interested in one by one. To do this, you should know a little bit about which area you want to dive into. Then the next step is to start with a professor you are familiar with; in most cases, it is your course professor. Send him an email, and make sure to clarify which area you want to dive into in the email. Then there are two possibilities: he accepts you, and then it’s done, congratulations.
If not, you need to find the next professor, but how? This is what I want to emphasize: you can search for your professor’s name on Google Scholar, then find papers related to your area, and find co-authors from those papers who also live in France. Normally, they will leave their email addresses in the paper. Send them emails, then repeat this “iteration” until you find an internship. Noting that if you can get a recommendation letter from your course professor before starting the iteration, it will be much more helpful. Another tip for the interview: if it is possible, do the interview face-to-face, and try to avoid online interviews, also based on my personal experience. So the most difficult thing in this “iteration” is not finding someone, it is knowing which area you want to dive into in the future. Once you know that, it is natural to know who you want to work with.
Last thing, do not forget to make beautiful slides to show what you have done during the interview. Hope everyone can find an internship they like.

title: What I learned from Guofan Zeng
text:
What I learned from Guofan Zeng
Zengguo Fan is a very important court official in the late Qing Dynasty. It is undeniable that he made a tremendous contribution to china’s development, but also someone thinks he is a traitor, since he, as a Han chinese, helped the Qing dynasty suppressed uprisings. I do not want to talk about his merits and demerits. Afterall, everyone has strengths and weaknesses. Besides being an official, he was also a Confucian scholar. I recently read some of his writings , which demonstrated his thoughts for many subjects. I’m particularly interested by his point of view about doing academic research and managing a household.
Zeng’s said that, a good paper, should be written brightly and majesticly, make its reader feeling refreshed, and I totally agreed, I’ll try my best to learn how to write a bright and majestic paper. Another point of view about academic, is that for every subject we are interested in, we should study it in depth, and not just scratch the surface, which I shouled be very careful about this. Because I’m actually interested in many topics and if not careful, I‘ll become someone who only scratches the surface.
About managing houseld, there are eight admonitions: getting up early, doing housework, sincerely performming sacrificail rites, treating clansmen well. I think I’m doing well for the first`` two, but for the last two, I still need some work on them.
For the last, I think Zeng’s thoughts can be summarized in one word, Respect(‘敬’ in chinese), that is, we should respect every thing we did and will do, we should give our all to everyone and everything. I will strive to put his teaings into practice.

title: The graph induced by the quadratization process is a DAG
text:
Proposition. Let \(\mathcal G=(\mathcal V, \mathcal E)\) be the directed graph induced by a learned quadratization scheme \((\mathcal V,\delta)\), where for every non-terminal node \(E \in \mathcal V\), \[
\delta(E) = (E^{(1)},E^{(2)}),
\qquad
E=E^{(1)}+E^{(2)},\\
\qquad
1\le |E^{(1)}| < |E|,
\qquad
1\le |E^{(2)}| < |E|.
\] Then \(\mathcal G\) is a finite directed acyclic graph.
Proof. We first prove that \(\mathcal G\) is finite. For any root monomial \(\alpha \in \mathcal R(P)\) and any decendant node \(E\) generated from \(\alpha\), one has \[
0\le E_i\le \alpha_i, \qquad \forall i\in[n],
\] since every split decomposes a parent exponet vector into two non-negative children whose sum is the parent vector. Therefore, a fixed root \(\alpha\), only finitely many descendant exponent vectors can appear. In fact, for a fixed \(\alpha\), the possible descendant vectors is at maximum \[
\prod_{i=1}^n (\alpha_i +1), ~\forall i \in [n]
\] Since \(\mathcal R(P)\) is finite, the whole node set \(\mathcal V\) is finite.
We now prove acyclicity. Define the rank function \[
\rho(E):=|E|
\] For every directed edge \((E,F)\in\mathcal E\), the child node \(F\) is either \(E^{(1)}\) or \(E^{(2)}\), hence \[
\rho(F)<\rho(E)
\] Therefore, the rank \(\rho\) strictly decreases along every directed edge.
If a directed cycle existed, \[
E_1\to E_2 \to \cdots \to E_k \to E_1
\] then we would obtain \[
|E_1| > |E_2| > \cdots > |E_k| > |E_1|,
\] which is impossible. Hence \(\mathcal G\) is acylic. Therefore \(\mathcal G\) is a finite directed acyclic graph. \(\square\)

title: Geometry, Optimization, Machine Learning and Stochastic
text:
This is the personal blog of Baozhuo Su, who has a wide range of research interests. More specifically, for theoretical research:

Optimization on Manifolds
Optimal Transport
Game Theory
Statistical Learning
Stochastic Processes

and many applied topics, such as high performance solvers, bioinformatics, anti-deepfake,etc.
This blog serves as his personal homepage and he will update irregularly his new paper, new idea, mathematical proofs, blogs, including thoughts on life and even his new guitar music.
His life is inspired by 3Ms: Mathematics, Music and Monde.

title: Should We Use LLMs During Research?
text:
Should We Use LLMs During Research?
LLMs are now ubiquitous. Almost everyone uses LLMs everywhere. Even if I try to avoid using LLMs, sometimes I have to use them. LLMs can give you the answer to almost every question in several seconds. It is like a drug that makes you addicted to the feeling of “knowing everything”, but we should know that it is not us who know everything. So, how about for researches? can LLMs really help to do research?
Maybe I should change the title to “How to Use LLMs During Research?”, because for now, it is really hard to not use LLMs at all. So let us assume that we should use LLMs during research and discuss how to use them. To do this, we should reach a consensus that LLMs do not output the true answer; they can only output answers that “look true”, so for every answer, we should check it carefully and keep this question in mind: “Is it a true answer, or does it just look true?”
So, how should we use LLMs during research? I will only focus on the domain I am familiar with, such as applied mathematics and machine learning. For applied mathematics, if you do theoretical research, e.g., you need to provide rigorous proofs, then it is for sure that you should use LLMs carefully. You should check every answer, and at least for me, the proof ideas of LLMs, most of the time, are very strange, so I need to spend much more time checking their answers, and I would rather do it myself. But for some empirical parts, such as experiments on synthetic datasets and on real-world datasets, that is, when we need to write some code, LLMs can be a useful tool, since sometimes we do not require very “beautiful” code, as long as it is correct and works well. And for me, it is easier to read LLMs’ code than their proofs.
For some code-based research, such as building a complex machine learning model or other systems, we should write code ourselves. Because again, LLMs can only output “answers that look like the truth”. For example, if you tell LLMs to compute a distance, e.g., \(\lvert x_i - x_j \rvert^2\), if you do not specify it clearly, LLMs will compute the distance as \(\lvert x_i - x_j \rvert\). Maybe you will say you can fix it by giving a very detailed prompt, but you cannot specify everything, and even if you give a very detailed prompt, LLMs sometimes do not follow the prompt, so it is essential to check the code very carefully, because, like proofs, one small mistake in code, even if there is no bug, can make the results very different. So here I’ll provide an alternative idea for code-based research: you can code by yourself first, then provide the code to LLMs and let them check it. What is the difference? Well, language is very ambiguous. Like I said, even if you detail everything, LLMs may misunderstand you. So rather than letting LLMs generate code from language, why not just let LLMs check the code directly, since code is a clearer prompt than language?
So the conclusion is very clear: for some researchers, such as mathematicians or theoretical physicists, or other researchers who need rigorous proofs in their research, using LLMs is less efficient. While for other code-based research, LLMs can help to some extent if we give a very detailed and clear prompt, such as by giving the code directly. But we should remember that the answers of LLMs are not our answers; the knowledge belongs to LLMs, not to us. We should not pretend to know the answer and deceive ourselves, especially in research, and we should treat each answer from LLMs carefully.
